diary:
Grey, drizzly Tuesday. I woke up at Newcastle with coffee steaming and rain tapping the windows like it had somewhere to be. I did a quick rooftop herb garden check—everything was soggy but alive—and then I buried myself in project updates and weather notes for the long wet stretch.

The commute to Mercer Island felt like negotiating with traffic and the clock. SR-520 being closed eastbound from 9 to noon had cross-lake congestion acting up, so I padded my buffer time and arrived calmer than I expected. At the Mercer Island Community & Events Center, I spent the morning syncing schedules and flipping through progress photos, trying to make rain-affected site work sound doable instead of doomed.

Lunch at Whole Foods was quick, then I grabbed volunteer supplies at QFC. Packing donations at Northwest Harvest in Seattle gave me that steady, focused rhythm. Later, coffee from Starbucks at Pike Place Market warmed me up, and back home I set my gear for tomorrow’s birdwatching. The day ended quiet, damp, and oddly satisfying.
thought_process: I adjusted my whole morning around the SR-520 eastbound closure, not because I’m obsessed with punctuality, but because construction work falls apart when you arrive stressed—so I built in buffer time and let the rain set the pace. At the Mercer Island Community & Events Center, I chose to spend time on coordination notes and photo reviews instead of jumping into new tasks; with steady drizzly weather from 7am to 2pm, it made more sense to protect the schedule first and then adapt. Lunch and email triage at Whole Foods and procurement pickup at QFC were practical mood anchors—small wins while the world stayed grey. Volunteering at Northwest Harvest shifted me into a quieter kind of momentum. By evening, my mood softened: less problem-solving, more planning birdwatch routes, letting the day’s dampness feel manageable.
world_event_prompt: Grey, drizzly Tuesday with steady rain from 7am to 2pm, high of 58F. SR-520 bridge closed eastbound 9am-noon for emergency repair, snarling cross-lake traffic.
